title: Pre recorded Lecture Systems Thinking Slides
body:
COMM3030 Social Entrepreneurship Practicum

Lecture: Systems Thinking

Dr Linh Nguyen, Course Convenor Lecturer UNSW Centre for Social Impact Email: linh-nguyen@unsw.edu.au   Acknowledgement of Country

We acknowledge the Traditional Custodians of the lands where we currently live, work and play. We pay our deep respects and give thanks to Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Elders past and present for their custodianship of Country.  Agenda • Systems and Systems Thinking • Systems Mapping • Where to Intervene?

Systems   System “an interconnected set of elements that is coherently organized in a way that achieves something…A system must consist of three kinds of things: elements, interconnections, and a function or purpose “

(Meadows 2009)

Image: Cambridge Institute for Sustainability Leadership (CISL) 5  Examples of Systems Environmental System Social System Biological System A coral reef ecosystem, A public education system The human body where where marine life, water shaped by teachers, DNA, tissues, cells and quality, climate impact students, administrators, muscles work together biodiversity and policies, and resources sustainability.

Health System Political System Economic System A healthcare system A government shaped by A market economy driven includes hospitals, medical institutions, laws, political by supply and demand, professionals, insurance parties, and citizens’ businesses, consumers, providers, and government participation and regulatory frameworks policies

Systems Thinking  What is Systems Thinking?

Systems thinking is a perspective of Systems thinking is a holistic method seeing and understanding systems as for understanding positive and wholes rather than as collections of negative influences on a problem and parts. A whole is a web of identifying the “big levers” for creating interconnections that creates emerging change (Ramina et al 2021, p.7). patterns (Peter Senge)

When one element of a system A strong systems approach reveals key changes, the other parts will be interactions, anticipates consequences, affected and, in the end, the stability of and guides strategic action. the whole system.

What is Systems Thinking? Considering all aspects of a system's behavior within its broader environment

By focusing on parts of a system, we fail to understand the whole.

The Blind Men and the Elephant https://www.theblindelephant.com/   Lacking a Systems Thinking… the solution to one problem can easily lead to a new and bigger problem later on or somewhere else in the system   Lacking a Systems Thinking… the solution to one problem can easily lead to a new and bigger problem later on or somewhere else in the system

• Case Study: Plastic Bag Ban Without Systems Thinking • Policy Goal: Reduce litter via single-use plastic bag bans • Outcome: ➢ Minor drop in plastic bag use ➢ Offset by rise in purchases of other plastic bags (e.g. bin liners) ➢ No significant reduction in litter ➢ Increased costs for low-income households ➢ Public frustration weakened support for environmental policies

• Systems Thinking approach would include: ➢ Understanding consumer behavior ➢ Evaluating lifecycle of alternative bags ➢ Assessing waste management capacity

• Complementary strategies: ➢ Promote truly reusable bags ➢ Public education campaigns ➢ Invest in biodegradable alternatives ➢ Improve recycling infrastructure

• Key Takeaway: ➢ Narrow bans can backfire. ➢ A systems approach anticipates ripple effects and enables sustainable, equitable solutions. Based on Macintosh et al. (2020)

More readings: Plastic Bag Bans Have Lingering Impacts, Even After Repeals | School of Business In banning plastic bags we need to make sure we’re not creating new problems Plastic bag bans: Lessons from the Australian Capital Territory - ScienceDirect  A problem in the system is everyone’s problem  Key Benefits of Systems Thinking • Connects knowledge across domains; see relationships between seemingly unrelated concepts.

Learning • Identify recurring patterns • Understand deeply the "why" behind phenomena. • Example: Medical students using systems thinking don't just see symptoms but understand how body systems interact to produce those symptoms.

• Identifies leverage points

Improving • Prevents unintended consequences; anticipate how changes might affect the whole system • Breaks persistent problems • Example: Tackling obesity by addressing food access, urban design, and socioeconomic factors.

• Navigate complex transformations by identifying interdependencies.

Transforming • Enable innovation, fosters sustainable solutions • Enable long-term change by redesigning systems. • Example: Cities go beyond recycling by integrating renewable energy and sustainable transport.

Source: Stanford PACS Center on Philanthropy and Civil Society  Systems Thinking helps us to … Shift the focus from events and behavioral patterns (which are symptoms of problems) toward systemic structures and the underlying mental models that shape them.

Source: Senge (1996)  Systems Thinking helps us to … … find the most important places for intervention to change the long‐term behaviour of a system.

Source: Senge (1996) 15  Systems thinking is seeking to understand the connections among elements in the system ▪ What depends on what? ▪ What is causing what? ▪ Where are information flows? ▪ Where control decisions are made? ▪ What information flows are critical? ▪ How to best engage stakeholders? ▪ How best to manage or intervene in the system for desired results?

Example: Application of systems thinking – Urban mobility Urban Mobility Challenges

Current Focus: Electric Vehicles (EV) Adoption Advances technology but does not transform the system Requires infrastructure changes (charging stations)

A Systems Thinking Approach

Urban Spatial Planning Modal Shift Enable Mobility Flexibility Design cities with homes, workspaces, Encourage public transport, Work from home, virtual and leisure areas within walking walking, and cycling meetings distance Reduce reliance on private cars E-commerce Improve public transport accessibility

Outcome: Smarter, More Sustainable Urban Mobility

Source: Adapted from Dr Theo Hacking, Cambridge Institute for Sustainability Leadership (CISL) 17  T hink systematically If we don’t think systematically when planning for change, sometimes we misinterpret what’s going on and what needs to be ‘fixed’

Systems Mapping  Systems Mapping Definition: Systems mapping is a visual representation of the components of a system and their interactions. It helps in understanding the complexity and interdependencies within a system. Usage: ▪ Provides tools to see the whole picture. ▪ To identify key stakeholders, processes and outcomes. ▪ Navigate its complexities by considering context and interacting factors like economics, politics and global trends. ▪ Diagnose problems and identifying leverage points for interventions.  Systems Mapping

Systems mapping helps analyze how a system functions and identify intervention opportunities, bridging the ‘Understand’ and ‘Design’ phases. It examines system components and their relationships, using two main map types:

Actor maps show people or organizations in a system, including stakeholder maps.

Factor maps highlight causes and effects, such as policies, processes, behaviors and beliefs.

Actor Maps Stakeholder Maps  Stakeholder Maps Example ▪ Visually represent stakeholders and their relationships, ▪ Help in understanding and managing complex systems by identifying key players and their potential influence Key Elements of a Stakeholder Map: ▪ Identifying Stakeholders: Determine who the key stakeholders are, both internal and external. ▪ Categorization: Group stakeholders based on their influence, interest and impact on the project or system. ▪ Prioritization: Identify which stakeholders require the most attention and engagement. ▪ Relationships: Show how stakeholders interact with each other and with the project/system. ▪ Visual Representation: Use diagrams, charts, or other visual tools to map the stakeholders and their relationships.

(IDEO U, n.d., What is a Systems Map?) 23  Stakeholder Maps Example Key stakeholders and their relationships: five primary stakeholders (Teachers, Principals, Students, Families, and the Local School Board) and shows how they relate to each other through directional arrows.

Types of relationships: The map uses symbols to denote different types of interactions:

▪ Heart symbols ( ): caring relationships or emotional support (connecting Teachers→Students, Principals→Students, and Families→Students)

▪ Information symbols (i): knowledge or information flow (connecting Teachers→Principals and to Students)

▪ Dollar symbols ($): financial resources or budgetary influence (connecting Local School Board→Principals and to Teachers)

Directional influence: The arrows illustrate how influence flows through the system, showing that some relationships are unidirectional while others involve mutual influence.

Systemic layers: suggests different layers of the educational system, from governance (School Board) to administration (Principals) to implementation (Teachers) to the ultimate beneficiaries (Students), with Families providing external support.

Feedback loops: between Teachers, Principals and Students, illustrating how actions in one part of the system can cycle back through other components.

(IDEO U, n.d., What is a Systems Map?) 24   Factor Maps Cluster Diagrams Multiple C ause D iagrams  Mapping Tool 1: Cluster Diagrams

▪ Similar to a mind map ▪ Serves as a starting point for factor maps. ▪ Helps visualize key factors around an issue and their connections. ▪ Begins with the central topic, then brainstorm related causes, consequences, and influences.

(New Philanthropy Capital (NPC), n.d.)   Mapping Tool 2: Multiple cause diagrams

▪ Provide an overview of the connections and causes in a system. ▪ Are often used externally to share a summary of a systems analysis.

Example: a multi cause diagram the failure of a ‘get tough’ policy on sick leave at work

▪ Strict Policies → Reluctance to take sick leave ▪ Employees fear consequences, so they come to work sick (e.g flu). ▪ Infected employees → Flu spreads ▪ Sick employees infect colleagues, increasing flu cases. ▪ Increased workload & stress → Weaker immune systems ▪ More work and stress impair health, leading to longer recovery times and chronic illness. ▪ More sick employees → Productivity decrease ▪ As more workers fall ill, productivity declines -> the policy’s goal fails

(New Philanthropy Capital (NPC), n.d.)  Systems Mapping: Pros & Cons Benefits Limitations ▪ Understand system dynamics ▪ Subjectivity: Maps reflect contributors’ perspectives, making diverse participation ▪ Collaborative process essential. ▪ Engaging communication tool ▪ Risk of oversimplification: Generalizing ▪ Strategic insights for action experiences can overlook important nuances, such as differences in racial or socioeconomic impacts. ▪ Provides a comprehensive view of the system, promotes holistic ▪ Complexity & expertise required: Effective mapping thinking. can be time-consuming and demands knowledge of systems thinking.

Where to Intervene?  The Spectrum of Interventions in a System Focus on Symptoms: may address visible, urgent symptoms but this narrow focus, can miss deeper, systemic issues. Treating Symptoms vs. Root Causes: Solutions targeting immediate causes may provide short-term relief but fail to deliver lasting change if underlying problems remain unaddressed. Systems Change: Sustainable solutions require tackling root causes, but this involves: ▪ Navigating complex, interconnected factors ▪ Changing entrenched behaviors ▪ Overcoming political resistance ▪ Investing significant time and effort

Source: Stanford PACS Center on Philanthropy and Civil Society 30  Example: The Spectrum of Interventions in a System: Food Security Solutions at Different Levels 1. A Focus on Symptoms 2. Remedying Symptoms 3. Systems Change (Longand Immediate Causes (Mitigating Effects) Term, Transformative) (Short-Term, Reactive) Example: Food banks and Example: A social enterprise Example: Community-based soup kitchens provide free offers affordable meal regenerative agriculture and meals. subscriptions using surplus urban farming initiatives. Impact: Immediate relief food. Impact: Empowers local from hunger. Impact: Reduces food food production, improves Limitation: Does not waste and provides wages, and promotes fair food address the root causes of consistent access to meals. policies. food insecurity. Limitation: Hunger Solution: Sustainable, longpersists without addressing term reduction in food food security insecurity.

Systems Thinking & Social Entrepreneurship   Global Challenges Demand Systems Thinking & Systemic Solutions

Source: Budinich and Mones (2025)

Entrepreneurs and innovators must apply systems thinking to identify root causes rather than symptoms of complex problems while recognizing the interconnectedness of issues (Budinich & Mones 2025)
